sets.passage: Our class wanted to raise money for a children's hospital, so we decided to hold a charity (1)___. Some classmates lost interest when the work grew hard, but a small group of us refused to (4)___. We spent our weekends baking cakes, making posters and collecting donated (5)___ from families across the neighbourhood. On the day itself, I was secretly (6)___ that no one would turn up. To my (7)___, the playground slowly filled with people. Parents bought our slightly burnt cakes, children played our homemade games, and the coins in our donation box steadily (8)___. By the end of the afternoon, we had raised far more money than we had ever (9)___ possible. But the day gave us something beyond the (10)___. Strangers had come together for a common (11)___, and our whole class felt a new sense of (12)___ in what we had achieved. When we handed the money to the hospital, a nurse told us how many sick children it would (13)___. In that moment, every tiring weekend felt completely (14)___. I learned that real change does not require great power or wealth; it only needs ordinary people willing to (15)___ together for something good.

sets.passage: One busy afternoon at the train station, I noticed a little boy standing alone, his face wet with (1)___. sets.passage: When I was thirteen, my aunt gave me a plain notebook and suggested I keep a (1)___. I thought it was a boring idea and almost did not (2)___. But to be polite, I wrote a few lines that night, and a few more the next, and without quite meaning to, I had begun a habit that would (3)___ my life. At first my entries were dull lists of what I had (4)___ each day. But slowly, the notebook became a place where I could be completely (5)___, writing thoughts I would never have spoken aloud. On hard days, putting my worries into words somehow made them feel (6)___. On happy days, writing helped me hold on to moments that would otherwise have been (7)___. Over the years, the single notebook grew into a shelf of them. Reading old entries, I could watch myself (8)___, and see how problems that once seemed huge had quietly been (9)___. Writing every day also slowly made me a better (10)___; words came more easily, and I began to enjoy (11)___ ideas clearly. In time, that childhood habit grew into a real (12)___ to become a writer. I do not know if I will ever publish a book, but I do know that the daily act of writing has made me more (13)___ of my own life and feelings. sets.passage: For my sixteenth birthday, instead of asking for a new phone or expensive clothes, I made an unusual (1)___: to give up single-use plastic for a whole month. My friends thought I was a little (2)___, but the idea had taken hold of me after I saw photographs of a beach buried in plastic (3)___. The first days were surprisingly (4)___. Plastic, I quickly discovered, was absolutely everywhere — wrapped around my food, my drinks, even my toothbrush. I had to (5)___ my whole routine. I carried a cloth bag and a metal bottle, refused plastic straws, and learned to (6)___ shops that sold loose fruit and vegetables. Some shopkeepers looked at me strangely; a few were genuinely (7)___ and helpful. It was not always (8)___, and once or twice I slipped up and accepted a plastic bag out of habit. But each small (9)___ taught me something. By the end of the month, I had thrown away only a fraction of the rubbish I (10)___ would. More importantly, my eyes had been (11)___. I now noticed plastic everywhere, and could no longer pretend the problem did not (12)___ me. The experiment did not save the planet, of course, but it (13)___ me, and quietly influenced my family and friends, several of whom began making small changes of their (14)___. sets.passage: I was never a confident child. Convinced that I was simply not (1)___ enough, I stayed silent in class and avoided every challenge for fear of (2)___.
